jacinth_chen.name: Jacinth Chen
jacinth_chen.summary:
Jacinth Chen is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U19 level, with 5 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Jacinth competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Jacinth has formed effective partnerships:
  • Youchang Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Jacinth isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
jacinth_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jacinth Chen, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jacinth is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 NorCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Jacinth has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jacinth saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Jacinth has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jacinth has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jacinth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U19, which means Jacinth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Jacinth is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jacinth wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jacinth, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

jack_richards.name: Jack Richards
jack_richards.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Jack Richards has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 18 tournaments and 50 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Jack keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Jack is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jack especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries).

With 18 different doubles partners over the years, Jack adapts to anyone:
  • Charvi Srivastava (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Kaylie Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Jack is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Jack is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jack has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Jack isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Jack has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jack_richards.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jack Richards, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 50 career entries in 18 tournaments, Jack has 2 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 24 entries where Jack drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 50 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jack saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 Egret Midwest CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jack's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jack experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jack has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, where Jack entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jack does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Jack has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 2 wins means Jack is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jack plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jack can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Jack has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jack's texts. The go-to partner is Charvi Srivastava (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Kaylie Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jack's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jack entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jack's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jack and said 'nah.'

One thing nobody can accuse Jack of is playing it safe. Jack has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Jack has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jack has built a permanent residence on that line. Jack has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jack has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jack showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Jack traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Jack entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jack paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 50 entries, and 48 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jack looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jack's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jack has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jack. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jack_yin.name: Jack Yin
jack_yin.summary:
Out of California, Jack Yin is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Jack in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Jack is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 BD) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U17) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Jack has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Weslie Chen — a trusted BD/U17 partner (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Jack has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jack has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jack brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jack_yin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jack Yin, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 21 career entries in 8 tournaments, Jack has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Jack drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 21 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jack saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jack would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Jack has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Jack has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jack's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jack plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jack has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Weslie Chen: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Weslie Chen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Isabelle Chen has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jack entered 3 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Jack's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jack and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Jack 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jack is not just losing locally — Jack is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yin family travels cross-country so Jack can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jack has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jack showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Jack traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Jack entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Jack paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jack keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jack has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jack, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jack. We will be here, taking notes.

jacob_ma.name: Jacob Ma
jacob_ma.summary:
Representing Illinois, Jacob Ma has climbed to the top of the mountain at Junior Nationals, the crown jewel of the American junior badminton season in BS U13. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 9 career victories across 24 tournaments, Jacob has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Jacob is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (7 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jacob especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 4 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (5 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (BS U13) • 4 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Jacob boasts a commanding 72% win rate across 114 matches, 24 three-game battles — proving Jacob is no stranger to the pressure of a decider, a biggest win margin of 34 points.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Jacob adapts to anyone:
  • Lexuan Wen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Jacob Zhou (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD)
  • Erica Chen Lin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in Mixed/XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Srineet Atluri (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 17 podium finishes across 7 tournaments. Jacob is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Jacob has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jacob has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jacob has been seeded #1 or #2 in 11 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jacob brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jacob_ma.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jacob Ma, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. And yes, Jacob IS a Junior National Champion — BS U13 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 69-entry career across 24 tournaments with a 13% win rate, which means 60 times — 60! — Jacob went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jacob saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, where Jacob burst onto the scene with a title in BS U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jacob has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jacob promised and what Jacob has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Jacob actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 19 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jacob experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jacob has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Egret MW CRC, where Jacob entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17, BD U17). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jacob does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Jacob has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 9 wins means Jacob is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jacob plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jacob can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Jacob has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jacob's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Sophia Sun: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sophia Sun has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Vishnu Anand has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jacob entered 5 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jacob's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jacob and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Jacob has been seeded in 20 events and underperformed the seeding 7 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jacob takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U15, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jacob. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Jacob of is playing it safe. Jacob has entered older age groups at 7 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Jacob has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jacob has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jacob is not just losing locally — Jacob is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ma family travels cross-country so Jacob can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jacob has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jacob showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U15, which means Jacob traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Jacob entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Jacob paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 60 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jacob looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jacob's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jacob has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jacob. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jacob_p_mangunsong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jacob P Mangunsong, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jacob is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Jacob has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jacob saga began at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Jacob has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jacob has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jacob showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Jacob traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip.

Jacob is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jacob wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jacob, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

jacob_zhou.name: Jacob Zhou

Jacob is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Jacob competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 6 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 8 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U13 BS, U13 BD, U15 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U13, XD U13) • 14 ORC titles.

  • Terence Cai — a trusted BD/U13 partner (7 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 4 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Jacob is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jacob has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jacob has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jacob has been seeded #1 or #2 in 12 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 19 titles, Jacob is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where Jade actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jade is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jade would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Jade has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Jade has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jade's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jade plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jade is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jade and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jade needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Jade has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jade's texts. The go-to partner is Diksha Chandrasekaran (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Anthony Cheng Xu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jade's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jade has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jade has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jade is not just losing locally — Jade is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hu family travels cross-country so Jade can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jade has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jade showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Jade traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Jade entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Jade paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 45 entries, and 44 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jade looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jade's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jade has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jade. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jaden_chen.name: Jaden Chen
jaden_chen.summary:
Out of California, Jaden Chen has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jaden's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Jaden competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: ORC top-2 finisher.
jaden_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaden Chen, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jaden is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Jaden has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jaden saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jaden experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jaden has reached the finals 2 times — at 2023 Midwest ORC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Jaden has it in spades.

Jaden is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jaden wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jaden, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

jaden_d_souza.name: Jaden D'Souza

jaden_jin.name: Jaden Jin
jaden_jin.summary:
Hailing from California, Jaden Jin is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Jaden in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Jaden has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Jaden especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U17 BD, U17 BS) • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U19) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jaden has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Thanik Bharani (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Thomas Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in U19 (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Akshaya A. Ajan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Michael Jiang — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Jaden has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jaden has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jaden has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jaden brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaden_jin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaden Jin, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 41 career entries in 19 tournaments, Jaden has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 40 entries where Jaden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 41 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaden saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 7th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jaden experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jaden has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Jaden has ZERO titles. None. 7 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 41 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jaden has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Adam Tay: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Adam Tay has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BD U17 — we hope Thanik Bharani has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaden entered 6 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaden and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Jaden 5 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Jaden has been seeded in 12 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jaden takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, and finishing 65th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jaden. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Jaden has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaden is not just losing locally — Jaden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jin family travels cross-country so Jaden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaden has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Jaden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Jaden entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Jaden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 41 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jaden. Jaden just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jaden has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jaden. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jaden: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Jaden has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaden has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Jaden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Jaden entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jaden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jaden keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jaden has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jaden, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jaden. We will be here, taking notes.

jaden_seangmany.summary:
Hailing from California, Jaden Seangmany has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 21 entries across 14 tournaments, Jaden is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Jaden is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jaden especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (4 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Jaden has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jaden has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jaden brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaden_seangmany.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaden Seangmany, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 21 career entries in 14 tournaments, Jaden has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Jaden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 21 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaden saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaden entered 4 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaden and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jaden has shown up 4 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Jaden has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaden is not just losing locally — Jaden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Seangmany family travels cross-country so Jaden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaden has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U17, which means Jaden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Jaden entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jaden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 21 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jaden. Jaden just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jaden has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jaden. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jaden: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

A California competitor, Jaden Zhou has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 22 tournaments and 61 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Jaden keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Jaden has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Jaden especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Jaden has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 24 different players across the career:

  • Radhika Birur (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jaden has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jaden has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Jaden has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jaden_zhou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaden Zhou, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 61 career entries in 22 tournaments, Jaden has 2 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 29 entries where Jaden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 61 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaden saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jaden's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Jaden actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jaden is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jaden can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Jaden has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jaden's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Mang Ki Cheung has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jaden's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaden entered 9 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaden and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jaden has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Jaden has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaden is not just losing locally — Jaden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhou family travels cross-country so Jaden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaden has 38 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Jaden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jaden entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jaden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 61 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jaden looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jaden's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jaden has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jaden. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jaleesa_yu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaleesa Yu, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 47 career entries in 17 tournaments, Jaleesa has 3 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Jaleesa drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 47 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaleesa saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jaleesa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Jaleesa actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jaleesa is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaleesa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jaleesa is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jaleesa and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jaleesa needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Jaleesa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jaleesa's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Prutha Bharadwaj Ramaprakash: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Prutha Bharadwaj Ramaprakash has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jaxton Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jaleesa's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaleesa entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaleesa's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaleesa and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jaleesa has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jaleesa has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaleesa is not just losing locally — Jaleesa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yu family travels cross-country so Jaleesa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaleesa has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaleesa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Jaleesa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Jaleesa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Jaleesa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 47 entries, and 44 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jaleesa looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jaleesa's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jaleesa has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jaleesa. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

james_lai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for James Lai, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. James has 5 career entries across 3 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The James saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. James plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, James has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. James has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where James showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means James traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where James entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 25th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying James paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, James keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities James has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without James, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, James. We will be here, taking notes.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for James Xu, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The James saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. James has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers James's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aditi Srinivasan: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. At Junior Nationals, James has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

James has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and James has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like James. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, James: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
