janice_chen.name: Janice Chen
janice_chen.summary:
A California competitor, Janice Chen is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Janice in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Janice is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Janice competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 6 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U15 XD, U15 GD, U13 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U13) • 5 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Janice boasts a biggest win margin of 37 points, a clutch 63% win rate in three-game matches (12/19), a commanding 71% win rate across 86 matches.

Janice has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 17 different players across the career:
  • Stephanie Jiayi Song (current primary partner) — 12 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (26 total events in GD/U13/U15, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Mikael Chang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (15 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Jayden Mar — 2 events in XD, best: #3, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Janice is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Janice has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Janice has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Janice has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Janice is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
janice_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Janice Chen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. And yes, Janice IS a Junior National Champion — GD U13 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 98-entry career across 33 tournaments with a 7% win rate, which means 91 times — 91! — Janice went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 98 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Janice saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Janice's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Janice actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Janice experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Janice has reached 16 championship matches across this career. That is 16 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 16 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Janice entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11, GD U11). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (12 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Janice does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Janice has never won anything. But 16 finals losses versus 7 wins means Janice is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Janice plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 6 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Janice can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Janice has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Janice's texts. The most successful partnership is with Stephanie Jiayi Song — 26 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Stephanie Jiayi Song deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Stephanie Jiayi Song a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jonathan Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Janice entered 9 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Janice's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Janice and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Janice has been seeded in 16 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Janice takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U13, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Janice. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Janice of is playing it safe. Janice has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Janice has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Janice has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Janice is not just losing locally — Janice is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Janice can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Janice has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Janice showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Janice traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Janice entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 65th. That is an average placement of 24th, which is a polite way of saying Janice paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 98 entries, and 91 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Janice looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Janice's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Janice has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Janice. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jasmine_cheung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jasmine Cheung, from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jasmine's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jasmine keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jasmine saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jasmine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jasmine has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Edward Chang (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Edward Chang has emotionally recovered.

Jasmine has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasmine has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasmine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Jasmine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Jasmine entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Jasmine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jasmine keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jasmine has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jasmine, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jasmine. We will be here, taking notes.

The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jasmine and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The longest-running partnership is with Vivian Shi: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Vivian Shi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The La family travels cross-country so Jasmine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasmine has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasmine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jasmine entered 2 events with results ranging from 1st to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Jasmine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 19 entries, and 18 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jasmine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jasmine_yeung.name: Jasmine Yeung
jasmine_yeung.summary:
Hailing from California, Jasmine Yeung has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 GD, Mixed Doubles, Women's Singles, U17 XD, U17 GD, U15 GS, U19 GD, U15 XD, U19 XD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 27 career titles across 20 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Jasmine is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Jasmine is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 9 doubles titles, 9 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 GD, Mixed Doubles, Women's Singles, U17 XD, U17 GD, U15 GS, U19 GD, U15 XD, U19 XD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U19, GD U19, XD U17) • 17 ORC titles.

Jasmine has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Angela Ye (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 6 titles across 7 events in GD/U15, spanning 3 seasons
  • Zicheng Xu (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 4 events in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Arden Q Lee — 4 events in Mixed/U19/XD, producing 2 titles together
  • Audrey Chang — 3 events in GD/U19, producing 3 titles together

Jasmine has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jasmine has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jasmine has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 27 titles, Jasmine is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jasmine_yeung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jasmine Yeung, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Jasmine is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Jasmine has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Jasmine would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Jasmine, we kept reading — all 58 entries across 20 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 47% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Jasmine held up a trophy, there were roughly 1 other times Jasmine walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Jasmine saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Jasmine burst onto the scene with a title in GD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jasmine has delivered on some of those promises: 27 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jasmine promised and what Jasmine has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 9 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Jasmine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jasmine experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jasmine has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 South ORC, where Jasmine entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U17, XD U17, GD U19). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jasmine does own 27 actual titles, so it is not like Jasmine has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 27 wins means Jasmine is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jasmine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 9 doubles, 9 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jasmine can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jasmine has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jasmine's texts. The most successful partnership is with Jun Heng Lai — 10 events, 8 titles — and honestly, Jun Heng Lai deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Jun Heng Lai a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting.

Then there is the seeding situation. Jasmine has been seeded in 11 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jasmine takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, and finishing 3rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jasmine. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Jasmine of is playing it safe. Jasmine has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Jasmine has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 18 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jasmine has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jasmine is not just losing locally — Jasmine is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 47% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yeung family travels cross-country so Jasmine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jasmine has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jasmine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Jasmine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 27 titles to the name, 58 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jasmine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jasmine's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jasmine has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jasmine. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jason Gu, from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 82 career entries in 31 tournaments, Jason has 11 titles — a 13% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 6 entries where Jason drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 82 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jason saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jason's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 5 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Jason actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 4 wins in 22 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jason experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jason has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 NE MassBad OLC, where Jason entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jason does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Jason has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 11 wins means Jason is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 7 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jason can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Iris Kuan: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Iris Kuan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Iris Kuan has emotionally recovered.

Of Jason's 11 career titles, 9 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Jason feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jason's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jason and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Jason 7 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Jason has been seeded in 21 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jason takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jason. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Jason of is playing it safe. Jason has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Jason has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 11 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jason has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jason is not just losing locally — Jason is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gu family travels cross-country so Jason can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Jason entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 11 titles to the name, 82 entries, and 71 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jason looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jason's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jason has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jason. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jason has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jason_h_chuck.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jason H Chuck, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jason's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jason keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jason saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jason has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BD U13 — we hope Vishanth Vijay has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13), where Jason entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jason keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jason has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jason, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jason. We will be here, taking notes.

The Jason saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

What makes Jason especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U13 BS, U13 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jason has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jason has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Jason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jason_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jason Yang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 18 tournaments, Jason has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 48 entries where Jason drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jason saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jason would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 9 times Jason has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 9 times Jason has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jason's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 49 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Allen w Yang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Allen w Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Vivian Feng has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Jason's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jason and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jason has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jason is not just losing locally — Jason is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Jason can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Jason entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 49 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jason. Jason just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jason has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jason. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jason: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

jason_zhu.name: Jason Zhu
jason_zhu.summary:
Out of California, Jason Zhu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jason's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Jason has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Jason competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Jason adapts to anyone:
  • Leo Ye (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Jason has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jason has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jason_zhu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jason Zhu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jason has 37 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jason saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Jason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jason's texts. The go-to partner is Hannah Luyi Xiao (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Leo Ye has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jason has shown up 8 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jason has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jason is not just losing locally — Jason is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhu family travels cross-country so Jason can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jason has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Jason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jason entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Jason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 37 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jason. Jason just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jason has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jason. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jason: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jasper paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jasper keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jasper has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jasper, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jasper. We will be here, taking notes.

jaxton_zhang.name: Jaxton Zhang
jaxton_zhang.summary:
A California competitor, Jaxton Zhang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jaxton's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Jaxton has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Jaxton competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jaxton has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Jaleesa Yu — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jaxton has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jaxton has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jaxton brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaxton_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaxton Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jaxton has 25 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jaxton saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jaxton's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Jaxton actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jaxton is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jaxton experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jaxton has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jaxton does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Jaxton has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Jaxton is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaxton plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jaxton can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jaxton has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jaxton's texts. The go-to partner is Jaleesa Yu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Jaden Ke Hong Chen has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jaxton's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jaxton has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jaxton has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaxton is not just losing locally — Jaxton is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jaxton can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaxton has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaxton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U15, which means Jaxton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jaxton entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jaxton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 25 entries, and 23 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jaxton looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jaxton's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jaxton has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jaxton. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jay_chun.roast:

jayden_ma.name: Jayden Ma
jayden_ma.summary:
Hailing from California, Jayden Ma is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U11 level, with 5 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Jayden competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, NorCal, Jayden isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
jayden_ma.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jayden Ma, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jayden is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC, and so far, the only thing Jayden has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jayden saga began at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jayden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Jayden has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jayden has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jayden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11, which means Jayden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Jayden entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jayden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jayden keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jayden has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jayden, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jayden. We will be here, taking notes.

jayden_mar.name: Jayden Mar
jayden_mar.summary:
Hailing from California, Jayden Mar has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jayden's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Jayden has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jayden is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Jayden adapts to anyone:
  • Christopher Gunawan (current primary partner) — 22 events, 1 title together in BD/U13/U15/U17, spanning 5 seasons
  • Erica Chen Lin (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Maggie Zang (current partner) — 4 events, 1 title together in U13/XD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jayden has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jayden has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Jayden consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Jayden is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jayden_mar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jayden Mar, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 95 career entries in 34 tournaments, Jayden has 5 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Jayden drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 95 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jayden saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jayden's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Jayden actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 13 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jayden experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jayden has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Jayden does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Jayden has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Jayden is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jayden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jayden can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Jayden has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jayden's texts. The go-to partner is Christopher Gunawan (22 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Kezhen Tao has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jayden's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jayden entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jayden's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jayden and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jayden has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Jayden has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jayden is not just losing locally — Jayden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mar family travels cross-country so Jayden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jayden has 48 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jayden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Jayden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jayden entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jayden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 95 entries, and 90 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jayden looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jayden's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jayden has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jayden. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jaylen_zhou.name: Jaylen Zhou
jaylen_zhou.summary:
A California competitor, Jaylen Zhou is a committed competitor who keeps showing up, currently competing at the U15 level, with 38 career entries across 15 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Jaylen's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

What makes Jaylen especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Jaylen has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Charlie W Gao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U15 (13 events, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Alice Luo — a trusted XD partner (8 events, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jaylen has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jaylen has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Jaylen brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jaylen_zhou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jaylen Zhou, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 38 career entries in 15 tournaments, Jaylen has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 37 entries where Jaylen drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 38 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jaylen saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jaylen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 38 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Charlie W Gao: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Charlie W Gao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Alice Luo has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jaylen entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jaylen's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jaylen and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jaylen has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jaylen is not just losing locally — Jaylen is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhou family travels cross-country so Jaylen can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jaylen has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jaylen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Jaylen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Jaylen entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jaylen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 38 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jaylen. Jaylen just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jaylen has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jaylen. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jaylen: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
