jeffrey_chang.name: Jeffrey Chang
jeffrey_chang.summary:
A California competitor, Jeffrey Chang is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Jeffrey in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

What makes Jeffrey especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 BD, U17 BS) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 5 ORC titles.

Jeffrey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Kai Chong (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Andrea Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

The 2023-2024 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Jeffrey is still ascending.

Jeffrey has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jeffrey has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Jeffrey consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 6 titles, Jeffrey has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jeffrey_chang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jeffrey Chang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 39 career entries in 13 tournaments, Jeffrey has 6 titles — a 15% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 5 entries where Jeffrey drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 39 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jeffrey saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2021 NorCal CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jeffrey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Jeffrey actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 9 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jeffrey experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jeffrey has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NorCal CRC, where Jeffrey entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jeffrey does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Jeffrey has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 6 wins means Jeffrey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jeffrey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jeffrey can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Jeffrey has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Kai Chong (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Alice Y Wang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jeffrey entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jeffrey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jeffrey and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jeffrey has shown up 3 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jeffrey is not just losing locally — Jeffrey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chang family travels cross-country so Jeffrey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jeffrey has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jeffrey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Jeffrey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Jeffrey entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Jeffrey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 39 entries, and 33 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jeffrey looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jeffrey's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jeffrey has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jeffrey. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jennie_gai.name: Jennie Gai
jennie_gai.summary:
Jennie Gai has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 2 career titles across 1 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Jennie competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles) — selected to represent the USA.

In doubles, Jennie has formed effective partnerships:
  • Joshua Yuan (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in Mixed
jennie_gai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jennie Gai, from parts unknown. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Jennie is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Jennie has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Jennie would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Jennie, we kept reading — all 2 entries across 1 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 100% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Jennie held up a trophy, there were roughly 0 other times Jennie walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Jennie saga began at 2021 Pan Am Junior Selection, where Jennie burst onto the scene with a title in Girl's Singles — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jennie has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jennie promised and what Jennie has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

Jennie is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jennie wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jennie, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

jennifer_bangcaya.name: Jennifer Bangcaya
jennifer_bangcaya.summary:
Hailing from California, Jennifer Bangcaya is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GD U15, GD U17, XD U17) puts Jennifer in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Jennifer has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jennifer is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U15, GD U17, XD U17) • 1 ORC title.

Jennifer has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Maggie Zang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (10 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Vincent Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Ethan Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Jennifer has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jennifer has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 19 seeded entries across the career, Jennifer consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 3 titles, Jennifer has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jennifer_bangcaya.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jennifer Bangcaya, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 95 career entries in 33 tournaments, Jennifer has 3 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 30 entries where Jennifer drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 95 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jennifer saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jennifer's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Jennifer actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jennifer is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jennifer experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jennifer has reached 10 championship matches across this career. That is 10 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 10 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NW ORC, where Jennifer entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jennifer does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Jennifer has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 3 wins means Jennifer is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jennifer plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jennifer is 0-for-29. Zero titles in 29 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jennifer and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jennifer needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Jennifer has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jennifer's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Maggie Zang: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Maggie Zang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Katie Wong has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jennifer entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jennifer's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jennifer and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 12 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Jennifer has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell.

Jennifer has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jennifer is not just losing locally — Jennifer is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bangcaya family travels cross-country so Jennifer can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jennifer has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jennifer showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Jennifer traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Jennifer entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Jennifer paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 95 entries, and 92 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jennifer looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jennifer's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jennifer has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jennifer. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jeremy_wang.name: Jeremy Wang
jeremy_wang.summary:
A California competitor, Jeremy Wang has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BD U11. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Jeremy belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Jeremy has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Jeremy especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U11) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Jeremy has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Leslie Wang (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (14 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Joanna Deng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Aayush Jaladurgam (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Jeremy is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Jeremy has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jeremy has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jeremy brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jeremy_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jeremy Wang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jeremy has 50 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jeremy saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC), where Jeremy burst onto the scene with a title in BS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jeremy has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Jeremy promised and what Jeremy has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jeremy experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jeremy has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC, where Jeremy entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jeremy does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Jeremy has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 3 wins means Jeremy is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jeremy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jeremy can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jeremy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jeremy's texts. The go-to partner is Leslie Wang (14 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Joanna Deng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jeremy's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. Junior Nationals has seen Jeremy 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Jeremy has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jeremy is not just losing locally — Jeremy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Jeremy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jeremy has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jeremy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Jeremy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Jeremy entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 65th. That is an average placement of 28th, which is a polite way of saying Jeremy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 50 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jeremy looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jeremy's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jeremy has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jeremy. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A true triple-threat, Jerry competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Hailey Hung (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Adam Tay (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Arthur Heng Lee — 5 events, 1 title together in BD/U19, across 2 seasons

Jerry has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

jerry_yuan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jerry Yuan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. And yes, Jerry IS a Junior National Champion — BD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 21-entry career across 10 tournaments with a 5% win rate, which means 20 times — 20! — Jerry went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 21 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jerry saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jerry burst onto the scene with a title in BD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Jerry has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 1 title in 3 entries — a stretch where Jerry actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jerry is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jerry would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Jerry has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Jerry has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jerry's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jerry plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jerry is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 17th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jerry and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jerry needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The longest-running partnership is with Hailey Hung: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Hailey Hung has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Arthur Heng Lee has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jerry entered 6 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jerry's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jerry and said 'nah.'

Jerry has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Jerry entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Jerry paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 21 entries, and 20 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jerry looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jerry's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jerry has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jerry. jeslin_vettikkatt.name: Jeslin Vettikkatt
jeslin_vettikkatt.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Jeslin Vettikkatt is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

A true triple-threat, Jeslin competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Jeslin has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Yelang Lee (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
jeslin_vettikkatt.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jeslin Vettikkatt, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jeslin is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Jeslin has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jeslin saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jeslin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jeslin has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jeslin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Jeslin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Jeslin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jeslin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Jeslin is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jeslin wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jeslin, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

jesse_lai_liu.name: Jesse Lai Liu
jesse_lai_liu.summary:
Out of Texas, Jesse Lai Liu is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U11 level, with 9 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Jesse competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Jesse has built chemistry with several doubles partners:

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Jesse paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jesse, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jesse. We will be here, taking notes.

jesse_sun.name: Jesse Sun
jesse_sun.summary:
Out of California, Jesse Sun is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 4 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Notably, Jesse has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jesse competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Jesse has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

jessica_wu.name: Jessica Wu
jessica_wu.summary:
Hailing from California, Jessica Wu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Jessica in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Jessica has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Jessica competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 XD, U15 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U17) • 1 ORC title.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Jessica adapts to anyone:
  • Ansen Liu (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in U19/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Chang He (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (16 total events in GD/U15/U19, spanning 5 seasons)

Having moved up to U19, Jessica is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Jessica brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Jessica has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jessica has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Jessica consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Jessica has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jessica_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jessica Wu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. And yes, Jessica IS a Junior National Champion — XD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 64-entry career across 22 tournaments with a 3% win rate, which means 62 times — 62! — Jessica went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 64 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jessica saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2024 Frisco South ORC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jessica's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Jessica actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jessica is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jessica experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jessica has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Jessica does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Jessica has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 2 wins means Jessica is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jessica plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jessica is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jessica and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jessica needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Jessica has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jessica's texts. The go-to partner is Chang He (16 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Caden Wang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jessica entered 12 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Jessica's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jessica and said 'nah.'

Jessica has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jessica is not just losing locally — Jessica is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Jessica can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jessica has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jessica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17, which means Jessica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jessica entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Jessica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 62 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jessica looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jessica's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jessica has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jessica. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jessica_zhang.name: Jessica Zhang
jessica_zhang.summary:
A Massachusetts competitor, Jessica Zhang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jessica's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Jessica is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Jessica is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 5 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Jessica boasts a commanding 71% win rate across 82 matches, a career +790 point differential, 49 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively.

Jessica has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 25 different players across the career:
  • Kingsley Chong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Zi Yang (current partner) — 5 events, 1 title together in GD, spanning 4 seasons
  • Megha Challa (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons
  • Bernice Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

Jessica has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jessica has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 17 seeded entries across the career, Jessica consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 13 titles, Jessica has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jessica_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jessica Zhang, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 76 career entries in 27 tournaments, Jessica has 13 titles — a 17% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 4 entries where Jessica drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 76 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jessica saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 MassBad NE OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jessica's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 6 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Jessica actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 16 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jessica plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 4 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jessica can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Jessica has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jessica's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Emily Min Tang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Emily Min Tang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Tao Peng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jessica's 13 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jessica entered 2 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Jessica's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jessica and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jessica has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5. Tournament committees have handed Jessica a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Jessica has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U15, where Jessica was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Jessica of is playing it safe. Jessica has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Jessica has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jessica has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jessica is not just losing locally — Jessica is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jessica can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jessica has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jessica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15, which means Jessica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Jessica entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Jessica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 13 titles to the name, 76 entries, and 63 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jessica looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jessica's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jessica has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jessica. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jessie's trophy case is so empty it echoes. jianxi_wen.name: Jianxi Wen
jianxi_wen.summary:
Representing California, Jianxi Wen is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 12 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Jianxi competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Jianxi has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Jianxi has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jianxi brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jianxi_wen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jianxi Wen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jianxi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jianxi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jianxi saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Jianxi has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Kezhen Tao has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jianxi is not just losing locally — Jianxi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wen family travels cross-country so Jianxi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jianxi has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jianxi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Jianxi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Jianxi entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jianxi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 12 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jianxi. Jianxi just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jianxi has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jianxi. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jianxi: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

jida_wu.name: Jida Wu
jida_wu.summary:
Out of Texas, Jida Wu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Jida's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Jida is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jida especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Jida boasts a solid 61% win rate over 97 matches, 15 three-game battles — proving Jida is no stranger to the pressure of a decider, a biggest win margin of 37 points.

Jida has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Liam Xiao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Mrugakshi Kulkarni (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Sienna Lee (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Harrison Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 7 tournaments. Jida is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jida has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jida has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jida brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jida_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jida Wu, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jida has 60 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jida saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jida's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jida would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Jida has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Jida has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jida's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jida plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jida can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Jida has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jida's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Liam Xiao: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Liam Xiao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Miura Yan Faye Goh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jida's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Jida has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Jida of is playing it safe. Jida has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Jida has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jida has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jida is not just losing locally — Jida is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Jida can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jida has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jida showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Jida traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jida entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jida paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 60 entries, and 58 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jida looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jida's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jida has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jida. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jishnu Sai Chennupati, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 21 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 21-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Jishnu's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Jishnu keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Jishnu saga began at 2023 South OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jishnu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Jishnu has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jishnu's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BD U15 — we hope Aarush Mali has emotionally recovered.

Jishnu has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Jishnu has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jishnu has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jishnu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U17, which means Jishnu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Jishnu entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jishnu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 21 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jishnu. Jishnu just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jishnu has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jishnu. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jishnu: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.
