joanna_deng.name: Joanna Deng
joanna_deng.summary:
A California competitor, Joanna Deng knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 63 entries across 22 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Joanna competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Joanna has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Crystal Yao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (5 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jeremy Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Sophia Coco Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Bliss Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joanna has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joanna has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Joanna has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
joanna_deng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joanna Deng, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 63 career entries in 22 tournaments, Joanna has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 62 entries where Joanna drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 63 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Joanna saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 OBA NW OLC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joanna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 24 entries — a stretch where Joanna actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joanna is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Joanna experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Joanna has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Joanna does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Joanna has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Joanna is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joanna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joanna is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joanna and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joanna needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Joanna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joanna's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Evan Lu: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Evan Lu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Zachary Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Joanna's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joanna entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Joanna's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joanna and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Joanna has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joanna is not just losing locally — Joanna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Deng family travels cross-country so Joanna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joanna has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joanna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Joanna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joanna entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Joanna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 63 entries, and 62 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Joanna looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Joanna's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Joanna has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Joanna. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

joannah_stephen.summary:
Out of Oregon, Joannah Stephen is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Joannah competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Joannah has formed effective partnerships:
  • Adelaide Almeida (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)

Joannah has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Joannah brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
joannah_stephen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joannah Stephen, from Oregon, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Joannah's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Joannah keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Joannah saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joannah has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joannah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15, which means Joannah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Joannah entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Joannah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 11 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Joannah. Joannah just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Joannah has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Joannah. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Joannah: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

joanne_li.name: Joanne Li
joanne_li.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Joanne Li has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 GD, U13 GD, U17 XD, U13 GS, U13 XD, U15 GS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 29 career titles across 26 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Joanne is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Joanne is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 8 singles titles, 8 doubles titles, 8 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 GD, U13 GD, U17 XD, U13 GS, U13 XD, U15 GS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U11, GS U11, GS U15, GD U13, GS U13) • 13 ORC titles.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Joanne adapts to anyone:
  • Kimaya Krishna (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 5 titles, building serious momentum (17 total events in GD/U13/U15, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Rithik Rasamsetti (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in U17/XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ethan Wu — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Terence Cai — 8 events, 1 title together in U13/XD, spanning 3 seasons

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 10 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Joanne is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joanne has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joanne has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Joanne has been seeded #1 or #2 in 10 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 29 titles, Joanne has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
joanne_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joanne Li, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Joanne is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Joanne has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Joanne would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Joanne, we kept reading — all 72 entries across 26 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 40% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Joanne held up a trophy, there were roughly 1 other times Joanne walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Joanne saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joanne burst onto the scene with a title in GS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Joanne has delivered on some of those promises: 29 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Joanne promised and what Joanne has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 11 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Joanne actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 5 wins in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Joanne experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Joanne has reached 14 championship matches across this career. That is 14 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 14 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19), where Joanne entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Joanne does own 29 actual titles, so it is not like Joanne has never won anything. But 14 finals losses versus 29 wins means Joanne is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joanne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 8 singles, 8 doubles, 8 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Joanne can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Joanne has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joanne's texts. The most successful partnership is with Kimaya Krishna — 17 events, 10 titles — and honestly, Kimaya Krishna deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Kimaya Krishna a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Yifan Liu has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Joanne has been seeded in 19 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Joanne takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 South CRC in GS U15, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Joanne. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Joanne of is playing it safe. Joanne has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Joanne has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 27 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Joanne has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joanne is not just losing locally — Joanne is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 40% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Joanne can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joanne has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joanne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15, which means Joanne traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Joanne entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Joanne paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 29 titles to the name, 72 entries, and 43 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Joanne looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Joanne's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Joanne has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Joanne. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jocelyn_youran_zhang.name: Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang

Representing Washington, Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 31 tournaments and 88 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Jocelyn keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Jocelyn is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (9 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Jocelyn especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Jocelyn adapts to anyone:
  • Zimeng Zhou (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (16 events, best: #5, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Daniel Seungchan Park (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (5 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Ethan Ao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Jaden Zhou — a trusted U15/XD partner (6 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

Jocelyn has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jocelyn has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Jocelyn consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Jocelyn has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
jocelyn_youran_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 88 career entries in 31 tournaments, Jocelyn has 2 titles — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 43 entries where Jocelyn drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 88 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jocelyn saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 NW OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jocelyn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 26 entries — a stretch where Jocelyn actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jocelyn is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jocelyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jocelyn can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Jocelyn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jocelyn's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Zimeng Zhou: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Zimeng Zhou has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ethan Biren Fong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jocelyn's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jocelyn entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jocelyn's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jocelyn and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jocelyn has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Jocelyn of is playing it safe. Jocelyn has entered older age groups at 9 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Jocelyn has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jocelyn has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jocelyn is not just losing locally — Jocelyn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Jocelyn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jocelyn has 43 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jocelyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U15, which means Jocelyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jocelyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jocelyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 86 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jocelyn looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jocelyn's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jocelyn has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jocelyn. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jocelyn, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

joey_liu.name: Joey Liu
joey_liu.summary:
A California competitor, Joey Liu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Joey in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Joey has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Joey especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 XD, U15 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U15) • 2 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Joey boasts a commanding 76% win rate across 76 matches, a biggest win margin of 30 points, a career +618 point differential.

Joey has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • William Geng (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (10 total events in BD/U15, across 2 seasons)
  • Selena Wei — a trusted U15/XD partner (4 events, best: #2)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joey has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joey has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 5 titles, Joey has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
joey_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joey Liu, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. And yes, Joey IS a Junior National Champion — BD U15 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 69-entry career across 25 tournaments with a 7% win rate, which means 64 times — 64! — Joey went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Joey saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 5 titles in 27 entries — a stretch where Joey actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joey is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Joey experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Joey has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Joey does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Joey has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 5 wins means Joey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joey is 0-for-24. Zero titles in 24 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joey and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joey needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Joey has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joey's texts. The most successful partnership is with William Geng — 10 events, 3 titles — and honestly, William Geng deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send William Geng a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Rex Hong has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joey entered 3 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Joey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joey and said 'nah.'

Joey has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joey is not just losing locally — Joey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Joey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joey has 29 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Joey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Joey entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Joey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 64 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Joey looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Joey's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Joey has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Joey. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

joey_sun.name: Joey Sun
joey_sun.summary:
Hailing from California, Joey Sun is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U19 level, with 14 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Joey competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Joey has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Aaron Liao — a trusted BD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joey has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Joey brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
joey_sun.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joey Sun, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Joey's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Joey keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Joey saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Joey has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joey has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Joey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Joey entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Joey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Joey keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Joey has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Joey, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Joey. We will be here, taking notes.

joey_wang.name: Joey Wang
joey_wang.summary:
Hailing from California, Joey Wang stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Joey is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Joey competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Joey boasts 48 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a commanding 73% win rate across 90 matches, a career +679 point differential.

With 23 different doubles partners over the years, Joey adapts to anyone:
  • Arnav Satish (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (7 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Daisy Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #2)
  • Trisha Mehta (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Eddie Sun (current partner) — 3 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 4 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Joey is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Joey has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Joey has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Joey consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 8 titles, Joey is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
joey_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joey Wang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 73 career entries in 25 tournaments, Joey has 8 titles — a 11% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 8 entries where Joey drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 73 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Joey saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Joey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Joey experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Joey has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Joey does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Joey has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 8 wins means Joey is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Joey can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Joey has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Joey's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Arnav Satish: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Arnav Satish has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Ryan Li has emotionally recovered.

Of Joey's 8 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Joey feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joey entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Joey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joey and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Joey has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

One thing nobody can accuse Joey of is playing it safe. Joey has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Joey has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Joey has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Joey is not just losing locally — Joey is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Joey can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joey has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Joey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Joey entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Joey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 73 entries, and 65 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Joey looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Joey's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Joey has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Joey. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Jogesh keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Jogesh is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Jogesh has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:

  • Jennifer Xu — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Jogesh isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jogesh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

Jogesh has 24 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jogesh saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jogesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Jogesh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jogesh is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jogesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jogesh is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jogesh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jogesh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Jogesh has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Nathan Kankanala (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Eddy Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Jogesh's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Jogesh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jogesh entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Jogesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 24 entries, and 22 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jogesh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jogesh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jogesh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jogesh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jonah_chenfei_wong.name: Jonah Chenfei Wong
jonah_chenfei_wong.summary:
Hailing from California, Jonah Chenfei Wong has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 51 entries in 18 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Jonah especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Jonah has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 17 different players across the career:
  • Alan Ma (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U15 (14 events, best: #3, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Adithi Krishnan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jonah has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jonah has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jonah brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jonah_chenfei_wong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonah Chenfei Wong, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 51 career entries in 18 tournaments, Jonah has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 50 entries where Jonah drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 51 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jonah saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 51 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Jonah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonah's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alan Ma: 14 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 14 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alan Ma has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Netri Vasireddy has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonah entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonah and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonah has shown up 9 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonah is not just losing locally — Jonah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wong family travels cross-country so Jonah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonah has 45 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Jonah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jonah entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jonah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 51 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jonah. Jonah just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jonah has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jonah. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jonah: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

jonathan_huang.name: Jonathan Huang
jonathan_huang.summary:
Representing California, Jonathan Huang is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U19 level, with 3 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Jonathan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Jonathan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
jonathan_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Huang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jonathan is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Jonathan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jonathan saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Jonathan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U19, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Jonathan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jonathan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jonathan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jonathan. We will be here, taking notes.

jonathan_khyewuiy_tan.name: Jonathan Khyewuiy Tan
jonathan_khyewuiy_tan.summary:
Representing California, Jonathan Khyewuiy Tan is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 3 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Jonathan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
jonathan_khyewuiy_tan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Khyewuiy Tan, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jonathan is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), and so far, the only thing Jonathan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jonathan saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Jonathan is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Jonathan's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Jonathan is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Jonathan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip.

Jonathan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jonathan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jonathan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.
