jonathan_liu.name: Jonathan Liu
jonathan_liu.summary:
Representing Washington, Jonathan Liu is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U19 level, with 17 entries across 8 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Jonathan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Jonathan has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, South, Jonathan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Jonathan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jonathan_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Liu, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jonathan has 17 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jonathan saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Jonathan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Leonard Wang (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Jiongchen Gu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 4 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Jonathan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Jonathan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jonathan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jonathan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jonathan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jonathan. We will be here, taking notes.

jonathan_loh.name: Jonathan Loh
jonathan_loh.summary:
Out of California, Jonathan Loh has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 55 entries in 20 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Jonathan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Jonathan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Jonathan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Xiao-ze Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (16 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Sherlyn Mei (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Jonathan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jonathan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jonathan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jonathan_loh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Loh, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 55 career entries in 20 tournaments, Jonathan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 54 entries where Jonathan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 55 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jonathan saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 55 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Jonathan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Xiao-ze Yang: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Xiao-ze Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Ethan Xiao-ze Yang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonathan entered 2 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonathan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Jonathan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonathan is not just losing locally — Jonathan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Loh family travels cross-country so Jonathan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 55 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jonathan. Jonathan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jonathan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jonathan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jonathan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

jonathan_wongso.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Wongso, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jonathan is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 South Houston CRC, and so far, the only thing Jonathan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jonathan saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Jonathan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Jonathan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Jonathan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Jonathan. We will be here, taking notes.

jonathan_wu.name: Jonathan Wu
jonathan_wu.summary:
A New Jersey competitor, Jonathan Wu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Jonathan in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

What makes Jonathan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (11 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Jonathan adapts to anyone:
  • Luke Han (current primary partner) — 18 events, 1 title together in BD/U13/U15, spanning 5 seasons
  • Ruohan Joy Du (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Annabelle Claire Cheng (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Daniel Su (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

Jonathan has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jonathan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Jonathan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Jonathan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jonathan_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jonathan Wu, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 95 career entries in 33 tournaments, Jonathan has 5 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Jonathan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 95 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Jonathan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jonathan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Jonathan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Jonathan is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jonathan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jonathan has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jonathan does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Jonathan has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Jonathan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jonathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Jonathan is 0-for-30. Zero titles in 30 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Jonathan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Jonathan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Jonathan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jonathan's texts. The go-to partner is Luke Han (18 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Irene Yan has emotionally recovered.

Of Jonathan's 5 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Jonathan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Jonathan entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Jonathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Jonathan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Jonathan has shown up 11 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6. Then there is the seeding situation. Jonathan has been seeded in 14 events and underperformed the seeding 8 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Jonathan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #8 at 2025 Capital NE OLC in BS U15, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Jonathan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jonathan is not just losing locally — Jonathan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Jonathan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 44 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 95 entries, and 90 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jonathan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jonathan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jonathan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jonathan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jonathan has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jonathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Jonathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Jonathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Jonathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 57 entries, and 56 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jonathan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jonathan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jonathan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jonathan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jordan_lau.name: Jordan Lau
jordan_lau.summary:
Hailing from California, Jordan Lau is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the final four 6 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Jordan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Jordan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Lorin Leung (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (6 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)

Jordan has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jordan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Jordan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
jordan_lau.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jordan Lau, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Jordan has 51 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Jordan saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Jordan would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Jordan has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Jordan has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Jordan's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jordan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 51 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Jordan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jordan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lorin Leung: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lorin Leung has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Anson Yat Shun Fong has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Jordan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jordan is not just losing locally — Jordan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lau family travels cross-country so Jordan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jordan has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jordan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Jordan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Jordan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Jordan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 51 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Jordan. Jordan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Jordan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Jordan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Jordan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

joshua_j_yang.name: Joshua J Yang
joshua_j_yang.summary:
A California competitor, Joshua J Yang is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Joshua in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Joshua is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (Boy's Singles, Mixed Doubles) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U19).

In doubles, Joshua has formed effective partnerships:
  • Ashley Yang (current partner) — currently teaming up in Mixed/XD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Joshua Yuan — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
joshua_j_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joshua J Yang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. And yes, Joshua IS a Junior National Champion — BD U19 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 5-entry career across 2 tournaments with a 20% win rate, which means 4 times — 4! — Joshua went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 5 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Joshua saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joshua burst onto the scene with a title in BD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Joshua has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Joshua promised and what Joshua has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 1 title in 3 entries — a stretch where Joshua actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Joshua is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joshua plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joshua is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joshua and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joshua needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Joshua entered 2 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-2. The Selection committee looked at Joshua's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Joshua and said 'nah.'

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 5 entries, and 4 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Joshua looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Joshua's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Joshua has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Joshua. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

joshua_yuan.name: Joshua Yuan

Winning at the Selection Event (Mixed Doubles) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 3 career titles across 2 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

What makes Joshua especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (Mixed Doubles) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U19, BD U19).

Joshua has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Jennie Gai (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in Mixed
  • Allison Q Lee — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

joshua_yuan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Joshua Yuan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Joshua is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Joshua has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Joshua would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Joshua, we kept reading — all 4 entries across 2 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 75% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Joshua held up a trophy, there were roughly 0 other times Joshua walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Joshua saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Joshua burst onto the scene with a title in XD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Joshua has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Joshua promised and what Joshua has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 2 titles in 3 entries — a stretch where Joshua actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 1 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Joshua plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Joshua is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 17th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Joshua and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Joshua needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Joshua has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Joshua showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 4 entries, and 1 losses. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Joshua's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Joshua has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Joshua. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

julia_chan.name: Julia Chan
julia_chan.summary:
Hailing from New Jersey, Julia Chan is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Julia is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
julia_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Julia Chan, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Julia is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NE CRC, and so far, the only thing Julia has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Julia saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Julia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Julia has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Julia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U15, which means Julia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Julia entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Julia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Julia keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Julia has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Julia, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Julia. We will be here, taking notes.

julia_ruan.name: Julia Ruan
julia_ruan.summary:
Representing Washington, Julia Ruan broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 37 entries across 14 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Julia competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Julia has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Manuel Melwyn Alleluia (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Zimeng Zhou — 3 events in GD, best: #4
  • Elizabeth Hu — 4 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Julia has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Julia has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Julia brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
julia_ruan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Julia Ruan, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Julia has 37 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Julia saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Julia's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Julia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Julia is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Julia would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Julia has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Julia has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Julia's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Julia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Julia is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Julia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Julia needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Julia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Julia's texts. The go-to partner is Elizabeth Hu (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Julia's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Julia has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Julia is not just losing locally — Julia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ruan family travels cross-country so Julia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Julia has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Julia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Julia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Julia entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Julia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 37 entries, and 36 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Julia looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Julia's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Julia has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Julia. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

julia_zhang.summary:

Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Julia's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Julia competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Julia adapts to anyone:
  • Jonathan Alexander surya Putra (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Vincent Xiaoyuan Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Sarah Lin (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #4)

Julia has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Julia's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.

juliette_zhou.name: Juliette Zhou
juliette_zhou.summary:
Representing Illinois, Juliette Zhou has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 8 entries across 3 tournaments, Juliette is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Juliette is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Juliette has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Yunny Huang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

Juliette has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
juliette_zhou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Juliette Zhou, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Juliette is right on schedule. Juliette plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. Juliette is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Juliette wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Juliette, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

jun_heng_lai.name: Jun Heng Lai
jun_heng_lai.summary:
Out of California, Jun Heng Lai has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (Mixed Doubles, U17 XD, U17 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 15 career titles across 20 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Jun is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Jun is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 3 doubles titles, 9 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (Mixed Doubles, U17 XD, U17 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U19, BD U19, XD U17) • 10 ORC titles.

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Jun adapts to anyone:
  • Zicheng Xu (current partner) — 6 events, 1 title together in BD/U17, spanning 3 seasons
  • Sydney Li (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Nicole Krawczyk — 3 events, 1 title together in Mixed/U19/XD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 2 tournaments. Jun is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Jun has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Jun has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 11 seeded entries across the career, Jun consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 15 titles, Jun is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
jun_heng_lai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jun Heng Lai, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Jun is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Jun has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Jun would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Jun, we kept reading — all 57 entries across 20 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 26% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Jun held up a trophy, there were roughly 2 other times Jun walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Jun saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Jun's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 5 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Jun actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 6 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Jun experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Jun has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Jun entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Jun does own 15 actual titles, so it is not like Jun has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 15 wins means Jun is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jun plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 9 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Jun can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Jun has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Jun's texts. The most successful partnership is with Jasmine Yeung — 10 events, 8 titles — and honestly, Jasmine Yeung deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Jasmine Yeung a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Yeening Yap has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Jun of is playing it safe. Jun has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Jun has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 13 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Jun has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Jun is not just losing locally — Jun is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 26% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lai family travels cross-country so Jun can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jun has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jun showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Jun traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NW ORC, where Jun entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 65th. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Jun paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 15 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 42 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Jun looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Jun's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Jun has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Jun. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

jun_jie_sam_lai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Jun Jie (Sam) Lai, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Jun is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 HPBC NW ORC, and so far, the only thing Jun has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Jun saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jun plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Jun has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Jun has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Jun showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U19, which means Jun traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Jun is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Jun wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Jun, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

junjie_lao.name: Junjie Lao
junjie_lao.summary:
Hailing from California, Junjie Lao has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 17 entries across 8 tournaments, Junjie is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Junjie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Junjie adapts to anyone:
  • Derek Sun (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Junjie has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Junjie brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
junjie_lao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Junjie Lao, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Junjie's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Junjie keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Junjie saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Junjie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Junjie has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Derek Sun (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Derek Sun has emotionally recovered.

Junjie has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Junjie has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Junjie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Junjie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Junjie entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Junjie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Junjie keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Junjie has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Junjie, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Junjie. We will be here, taking notes.

A true triple-threat, Junwoo competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.
