  • Mingyang Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Akshath Potnuru (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Junwoo has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Junwoo has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Junwoo brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
junwoo_lee.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Junwoo Lee, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-49. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Junwoo has 43 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Junwoo saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Junwoo plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 43 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Junwoo has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Junwoo's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Tanvi Jandhyala: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Tanvi Jandhyala has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Tanvi Jandhyala has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Junwoo has shown up 3 times with a best of top-49. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Junwoo is not just losing locally — Junwoo is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lee family travels cross-country so Junwoo can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Junwoo has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Junwoo showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Junwoo traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Junwoo entered 3 events with results ranging from 49th to 65th. That is an average placement of 59th, which is a polite way of saying Junwoo paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 43 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Junwoo. Junwoo just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Junwoo has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Junwoo. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Junwoo: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

justin_dong.name: Justin Dong
justin_dong.summary:
Hailing from New Jersey, Justin Dong is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 3 titles across 22 tournaments and 61 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Justin keeps finding ways to come out on top.

What makes Justin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Justin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 17 different players across the career:
  • Henry Tan (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (10 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Crystal Yao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Jason Yiheng Luo (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Mrugakshi Kulkarni (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Justin has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Justin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justin_dong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin Dong, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Justin has 61 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Justin saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Justin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justin is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Justin experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Justin has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 NE OLC (NVBC), where Justin entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13, BD U13). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Justin does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Justin has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 3 wins means Justin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Justin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The go-to partner is Henry Tan (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Niyati Tamerissi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Justin's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Justin has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Dong family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 61 entries, and 58 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Justin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Justin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Justin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Justin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Justin has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Nicholas Foh (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15 — we hope Sandra Chen has emotionally recovered.

Justin has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Justin has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Justin keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Justin has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Justin, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Justin. We will be here, taking notes.

Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Justin, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

justin_john_george.name: Justin John George
justin_john_george.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Justin John George is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the final four 4 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Justin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (7 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Justin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Oliver Nguyen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Anushka Mandadi — a trusted XD partner (4 events)

Justin has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Justin isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Justin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
justin_john_george.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin John George, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 28 events across 12 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 28-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Justin's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Justin keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Justin saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Justin would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Justin has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Justin has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Justin's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 28 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The go-to partner is Anushka Mandadi (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Anushka Mandadi has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Justin of is playing it safe. Justin has entered older age groups at 7 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Justin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Justin has built a permanent residence on that line. Justin has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 28 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Justin. Justin just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Justin has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Justin. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Justin: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

justin_kan.name: Justin Kan
justin_kan.summary:
A California competitor, Justin Kan has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 3 career titles across 23 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Justin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Justin is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (BS U17, BD U17) • 2 ORC titles.

Justin has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Nihal Kadamba (current primary partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 3 titles across 22 events in BD/U15/U17/U19, spanning 5 seasons
  • Charity Lam — a trusted U15/XD partner (6 events, best: #4)
  • Samanvi Boppana — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Justin has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Justin consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Justin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justin_kan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin Kan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Justin is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Justin has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Justin would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Justin, we kept reading — all 58 entries across 23 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 5% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Justin held up a trophy, there were roughly 18 other times Justin walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Justin saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Justin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justin is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Justin experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Justin has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Justin does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Justin has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 3 wins means Justin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Justin is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Justin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Justin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The most successful partnership is with Nihal Kadamba — 22 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Nihal Kadamba deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Nihal Kadamba a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope Nihal Kadamba has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Justin has competed 5 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

One thing nobody can accuse Justin of is playing it safe. Justin has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Justin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Justin has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kan family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 97th. That is an average placement of 73rd, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 58 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Justin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Justin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Justin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Justin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The sport needs people like Justin. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Justin: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

justin_lim.name: Justin Lim
justin_lim.summary:
Representing California, Justin Lim has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Justin's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Justin competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Justin adapts to anyone:
  • Hardik Gowrish (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Justin has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Justin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
justin_lim.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin Lim, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Justin has 26 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Justin saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 26 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The go-to partner is Hardik Gowrish (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Sophia Liu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Justin has shown up 4 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lim family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 26 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Justin. Justin just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Justin has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Justin. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Justin: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

justin_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin Liu, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 23 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 23-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Justin's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Justin keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Justin saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 23 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Yiyang Xu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Yiyang Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sri Nidhi Vasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

Justin has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Justin keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Justin has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Justin, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Justin. We will be here, taking notes.

justin_xia.name: Justin Xia
justin_xia.summary:
Out of Illinois, Justin Xia has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U17 BD, U17 XD, U15 BD, U15 XD, U17 BS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 12 career titles across 30 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Justin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Justin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U17 BD, U17 XD, U15 BD, U15 XD, U17 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U15, XD U15, XD U19, BD U17, BD U19) • 5 ORC titles.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Justin adapts to anyone:
  • Cassidy Yeh (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in U17/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Evan Liu — 16 events in BD/U15/U17, producing 2 titles together, spanning 3 seasons

Justin has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Justin has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Justin has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 12 titles, Justin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
justin_xia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin Xia, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Justin is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Justin has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Justin would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Justin, we kept reading — all 88 entries across 30 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 14% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Justin held up a trophy, there were roughly 6 other times Justin walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Justin saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Justin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justin is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Justin experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Justin has reached 19 championship matches across this career. That is 19 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 19 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Midwest CRC, where Justin entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (5 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (11 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Justin does own 12 actual titles, so it is not like Justin has never won anything. But 19 finals losses versus 12 wins means Justin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Justin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The go-to partner is Evan Liu (16 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Prabhat Mandava has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 12 entries, 3 finals appearances, and zero titles. Justin has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 3 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Then there is the seeding situation. Justin has been seeded in 24 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Justin takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) in BS U17, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Justin. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Justin of is playing it safe. Justin has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Justin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Justin has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xia family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U15, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Justin entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 12 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 76 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Justin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Justin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Justin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Justin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Justin adapts to anyone:
  • Sarvesh Hariharasudhan (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)
  • Devaansh Mahato — 2 events in BD, best: #2

Justin has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Justin has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Justin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
justin_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justin Yang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Justin has 25 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Justin saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 25 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Justin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Justin's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Sarvesh Hariharasudhan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Justin has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Justin is not just losing locally — Justin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Justin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justin has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Justin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Justin entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Justin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Justin keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Justin has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Justin, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Justin. We will be here, taking notes.

justine_yong.name: Justine Yong
justine_yong.summary:
Hailing from California, Justine Yong broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 17 entries across 8 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Justine has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Justine especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Justine adapts to anyone:
  • Atticus Yong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #4, spanning 4 seasons)

Justine has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Justine has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
justine_yong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Justine Yong, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 17 entries in 8 tournaments. That is a 6% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Justine shows up to a tournament, Justine pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Justine saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Justine's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 4 entries — a stretch where Justine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Justine is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Justine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Justine is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Justine has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Atticus Yong: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Atticus Yong has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Atticus Yong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Justine's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Justine has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Justine has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Justine has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Justine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U17, which means Justine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Justine entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Justine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 17 entries, and 16 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Justine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Justine's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Justine has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Justine. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
